era_context: The late 17th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires; Louis XIV at the height of French power; the Ottoman Empire in decline after the failed siege of Vienna (1683); the Qing Dynasty under Kangxi consolidating control over China; the Mughal Empire in India under Aurangzeb in its final decades.

events.title: Louis XIV Revokes the Edict of Nantes
events.description: On 18 October 1685, King Louis XIV of France issued the Edict of Fontainebleau, which revoked the Edict of Nantes (1598). The Edict of Nantes had granted religious toleration and civil rights to French Huguenots (Protestants). The revocation ended religious toleration and initiated a period of persecution against Huguenots. The revocation led to the forced conversion of many Huguenots to Catholicism and the flight of thousands of Huguenots to Protestant countries such as England, the Dutch Republic, Prussia, and Switzerland. The revocation had significant economic and demographic consequences for France, as many skilled artisans and merchants left the country.
events.certainty_note: The revocation of the Edict of Nantes is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the period, with the edict itself preserved as a primary text.

events.title: Monmouth's Rebellion in England
events.description: In June 1685, James Scott, Duke of Monmouth, led a rebellion against King James II in southwest England. Monmouth, the illegitimate son of Charles II, claimed to be the rightful heir to the throne and sought to prevent the Catholic James II from consolidating power. The rebellion was defeated at the Battle of Sedgemoor on 6 July 1685. Monmouth was captured and executed. The rebellion was followed by harsh reprisals against the rebels, including the 'Bloody Assizes' conducted by Judge George Jeffreys. The rebellion demonstrated the religious and political tensions in England that would lead to the Glorious Revolution in 1688.
key_figures: James Scott, Duke of Monmouth (rebel leader)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by eyewitnesses and participants
English state papers and official records

events.certainty_note: Monmouth's Rebellion is well-documented in contemporary accounts and official records.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor Consolidates Control Over Southern China
events.description: By 1685, the Kangxi Emperor had fully consolidated Qing control over southern China following the suppression of the Three Feudatories Rebellion (1673–1681). The consolidation of power in the south allowed the Kangxi Emperor to pursue expansionist policies in Mongolia, Manchuria, and Central Asia. The year 1685 marked a turning point in the Kangxi Emperor's reign, as he shifted from consolidating internal control to expanding Qing territorial dominion.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and court diaries

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Kangxi's control over southern China is well-documented in Qing imperial archives.

events.title: Aurangzeb Continues Deccan Campaign
events.description: In 1685, the Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb continued his military campaign in the Deccan, fighting against the Maratha Confederacy and other regional powers. The campaign, which had begun in the early 1680s, was costly and ultimately unsuccessful in permanently subduing the Marathas. The Deccan campaign drained Mughal resources and contributed to the empire's decline after Aurangzeb's death in 1707.

sources.name: Mughal court records and official histories

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Deccan campaign is documented in Mughal records, though specific events and dates within the campaign are sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian War (Morean War) Continues
events.description: The Morean War (1684–1699) between the Ottoman Empire and Venice, with support from the Holy Roman Empire and Papal States, continued in 1685. The war involved campaigns in the Peloponnese, Crete, and the Aegean. Venice achieved significant territorial gains, particularly in the Peloponnese, though the war was costly for both sides. The conflict reflected the Ottoman Empire's declining military capacity and the rise of European naval power in the Mediterranean.

sources.name: Venetian state papers and military records

events.certainty_note: The Morean War is well-documented in Ottoman and Venetian sources.

events.title: English Succession Crisis: James II Consolidates Power
events.description: In 1685, King James II, who had ascended to the throne in February 1685 following the death of his brother Charles II, worked to consolidate his power and promote Catholic interests. James II's pro-Catholic policies and his attempts to expand royal prerogative alarmed Protestant nobles and politicians. The year 1685 marked the beginning of the political crisis that would lead to the Glorious Revolution in 1688. James II's actions in 1685, including the suppression of Monmouth's Rebellion and the promotion of Catholic officials, deepened the political divisions in England.

sources.name: English state papers and official records

events.certainty_note: James II's consolidation of power in 1685 is well-documented in English state papers and contemporary accounts.

events.title: Siamese-French Relations: French Embassy to Siam
events.description: In 1685, French diplomatic and military missions were active in Siam (present-day Thailand) under King Narai. The French sought to establish a foothold in Southeast Asia and to convert the Siamese court to Catholicism. French influence at the Siamese court was significant, with French advisors and military officers playing important roles. However, the French presence in Siam would decline after 1688 due to internal Siamese politics and the rise of anti-foreign sentiment.
events.certainty_note: French diplomatic activities in Siam are documented in French records, though the exact timeline and significance of events in 1685 are somewhat uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The revocation of the Edict of Nantes is often presented as a straightforward act of religious intolerance, but recent scholarship emphasizes that Louis XIV's motivations were complex, involving both religious conviction and political calculations about national unity. The Huguenot exodus, while significant, did not have the catastrophic economic impact on France that was once claimed. Monmouth's Rebellion is sometimes presented as a popular uprising against tyranny, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was primarily an elite political struggle, with limited popular support. The rebellion's failure was not inevitable, and James II's victory was partly due to luck and the incompetence of the rebels. James II's consolidation of power in 1685 is sometimes presented as a straightforward assertion of royal authority, but it was also a response to genuine political and religious divisions in England.
historiographic_note: 1685 is well-documented in European sources, particularly French and English records. Asian sources (Chinese, Ottoman, Indian, Siamese) are also available but are often underutilized in Western histories. The perspective is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; the experiences of common people, women, and non-European populations are largely absent. The Huguenot perspective on the revocation of the Edict of Nantes is well-represented in refugee accounts, but the perspective of French Catholics who supported the revocation is less well-represented. Indigenous perspectives on European colonial activities are almost entirely absent from surviving records.

graph_edges.note: The revocation of the Edict of Nantes in 1685 led to the enforcement of the revocation and the persecution of Huguenots in 1687
Monmouth's Rebellion in 1685 was a precursor to the Glorious Revolution in 1688, as both reflected opposition to James II
The consolidation of Kangxi's control over southern China in 1685 led to the conquest of Taiwan in 1686
The continuation of the Deccan campaign in 1685 led to the capture of Bijapur in 1686
James II's consolidation of power in 1685 and his pro-Catholic policies led directly to the Glorious Revolution in 1688
